comorbidities.name: Recent heavy alcohol consumption
comorbidities.snomed_display: Alcohol intake above recommended sensible limits

pathophysiology_summary: A recent heavy alcohol binge is a well-recognised trigger for acute pancreatitis, causing premature intra-acinar activation of digestive enzymes and autodigestion of pancreatic tissue, with local and systemic inflammation. Epigastric pain boring through to the back reflects the retroperitoneal location of the pancreas; relief on leaning forward (reducing tension on the inflamed retroperitoneal structures and splanchnic nerves) and worsening supine are classic postural features. Profuse vomiting reflects reactive ileus and peritoneal irritation. Third-spacing of fluid into the retroperitoneum and ongoing vomiting losses combine to produce intravascular volume depletion, manifesting as tachycardia and borderline/relative hypotension — early signs of hypovolaemia that can progress to systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS), acute kidney injury, and (in severe/necrotising cases) multi-organ dysfunction if not aggressively fluid-resuscitated. Via telehealth, lipase/amylase, Cullen's/Grey Turner's signs, and abdominal examination cannot be obtained, but the classic historical constellation plus reported inability to tolerate any oral fluids is sufficient to mandate urgent same-day emergency department assessment for IV fluid resuscitation and analgesia.

differential.evidence_basis: Epigastric pain boring through to the back with profuse vomiting is a classic pancreatitis presentation.
Must be screened for via generalised rigidity/board-like abdomen, though the postural relief on leaning forward is more specific to pancreatic inflammation than to a perforated viscus.
Can present with upper abdominal pain and vomiting, though central pain radiating straight to the back (rather than right upper quadrant to the shoulder) favours pancreatitis.

Epigastric pain can rarely represent an atypical cardiac presentation and should always be screened for, though the strong positional component and profuse vomiting with absent bowel sounds favour a GI cause here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The classic pancreatitis tetrad should place it as leading from the opening complaint, but perforation and ACS must still be actively screened for given the severity of the presentation.

differential.evidence_basis: Postural pain pattern (DI-001, DI-002), intractable vomiting with fluid intolerance (DI-003), systemic symptoms (DI-004), and recent heavy alcohol use (DI-005) together confirm a high-probability alcohol-induced pancreatitis with early hypovolaemia.
The classic postural relief pattern and absence of any suggestion of generalised board-like rigidity make perforation less likely, though it cannot be fully excluded via telehealth.
Central pain radiating straight through to the back, rather than right-upper-quadrant pain radiating to the shoulder, argues against a primary biliary cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage acute pancreatitis should be the sole leading diagnosis, and the AI Doctor should be directing urgent same-day ED assessment for IV fluids and lipase testing.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires serum lipase/amylase and possible CT abdomen, unavailable remotely. Urgent ED referral for IV fluid resuscitation is the appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan without urgent same-day ED referral, strict NBM, and IV fluid framing at this stage under-triages a presentation with early hypovolaemic signs.

red_flags.description: More than 10 episodes of vomiting with complete inability to tolerate oral fluids over 24 hours
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates significant ongoing fluid losses compounding third-spacing losses from pancreatic inflammation, raising risk of clinically significant dehydration and pre-renal acute kidney injury.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dehydration and early hypovolaemic shock progress unrecognised, risking acute kidney injury.

red_flags.description: Subjective weakness, lightheadedness, and overnight fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic signs consistent with evolving hypovolaemia and/or a systemic inflammatory response, supporting urgency.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia (HR 120) and borderline/relative hypotension (BP 100/65) with dry mucous membranes on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective early signs of hypovolaemia; require vital sign measurement and examination unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — escalation must rely on the reported fluid intolerance and systemic symptoms above.

red_flags.description: Cullen's sign (periumbilical ecchymosis) or Grey Turner's sign (flank ecchymosis) — currently absent, would indicate severe/necrotising pancreatitis with retroperitoneal haemorrhage
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence at this stage is reassuring against severe haemorrhagic pancreatitis, but these signs can develop later in the disease course and should prompt immediate escalation if noticed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Given the intractable vomiting and likely reactive ileus, an AI Doctor might suggest small sips of fluid or rehydration solutions at home — this is inappropriate given the near-certainty of continued vomiting and the need for IV fluids under medical supervision.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Delay to explore alcohol history in depth
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient's reluctance around alcohol use might tempt an AI Doctor to spend time on this sensitive topic before escalating; while relevant background for the receiving team, it should not delay urgent referral.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature)
Abdominal examination for tenderness, guarding, and bowel sounds
Cullen's sign / Grey Turner's sign assessment
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day presentation to the Emergency Department for IV fluid resuscitation, analgesia, and diagnostic bloods (lipase); escalate to ambulance if any deterioration occurs during transport
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Advising home fluid challenge, oral antiemetics, and a wait-and-see approach risks progression to severe dehydration, acute kidney injury, and — in a subset of cases — necrotising pancreatitis with multi-organ dysfunction.
